The naive calculation says the game gets worse as \(n\) grows. The correct calculation shows it becomes positive from \(n = 2\) onward (with a small dip at \(n = 3\)).
At \(n = 1\), both agree: the game has not been aggregated, so the nonlinearity of \(U\) has no effect. For \(n \geq 2\), the total outcome \(Z\) can be large and positive, where the concavity of \(U\) is less punishing than the factor-of-2 penalty for losses. The diversification across flips helps.
For linear \(U\): \(U(\sum Z_j) = \sum U(Z_j)\), so the two columns would be identical. Nonlinearity is what makes them diverge.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Properties of loss functions
1. Invariance under positive affine transformations.
Claim: If \(L\) is a valid loss, then \(L' = bL + c\) (with \(b > 0\)) leads to the same optimal decisions.
Proof (for the Bayes case): The Bayes risk is \(r(\pi, \delta) = E_\pi[R(\theta, \delta)]\). Under \(L'\): \[r'(\pi, \delta) = E_\pi[E_{P_\theta}[bL(\theta, \delta(X)) + c]] = b\, r(\pi, \delta) + c.\] Since \(b > 0\), the \(\delta\) minimizing \(r'\) is the same as the \(\delta\) minimizing \(r\). \(\blacksquare\)
So we can always shift: \(L^*(\theta, a) = L(\theta, a) - \inf_{\theta, a} L(\theta, a) \geq 0\).
2. Regret loss. \(\ell(\theta, a) = \sup_{a'} U(\theta, a') - U(\theta, a)\): the loss relative to the best action for the true \(\theta\). Equivalent to \(L\) for Bayesian analysis, but can differ for minimax. We return to this in Chapter 5.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
I. Squared-error loss
\[L(\theta, a) = (\theta - a)^2.\]
Why is it popular?

For unbiased estimators, \(R(\theta, \delta) = \mathrm{Var}_{P_\theta}(\delta(X))\).
Related to least squares theory.
Calculations are often tractable.

Berger’s honest assessment: “The above justifications really have very little merit. The question is – does squared-error loss typically reflect the true loss?”
Squared-error loss is unbounded and convex: large errors are penalized very severely. In many real problems, the loss should be bounded and (for large errors) concave.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: Example: risk of the sample mean under squared-error loss
text:
Example: risk of the sample mean under squared-error loss
\(X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\). Estimate \(\theta\) with \(\delta(X) = \bar{X}\).
\[R(\theta, \delta) = E_{P_\theta}[(\theta - \bar{X})^2] = \mathrm{Var}_{P_\theta}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}.\]
The risk is constant in \(\theta\) and decreases in \(n\).
Now consider \(\delta_c(X) = c\bar{X}\) for some \(c \in \mathbb{R}\). Then: \[R(\theta, \delta_c) = E_{P_\theta}[(\theta - c\bar{X})^2] = c^2 \frac{\sigma^2}{n} + (1-c)^2\theta^2.\]
This is Example 4 from Lecture 1 with \(\sigma^2/n\) replacing 1. Minimizing over \(c\) for a fixed \(\theta\) gives \(c^* = \theta^2/(\theta^2 + \sigma^2/n)\), which depends on \(\theta\) – so the “optimal shrinkage” is unknowable. This tension is at the heart of the course.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: The Taylor expansion defense of squared-error loss
text:
The Taylor expansion defense of squared-error loss
Suppose the reward is \(Z = h(\theta - a, Y)\), where \(Y\) is an extraneous random variable (e.g., state of the economy) independent of \(\theta\) and \(a\).
Define \(g(\theta - a, Y) = U(h(\theta - a, Y))\). If the sample information is accurate, \(\theta - a\) is small. Expand in Taylor series around 0: \[g(\theta - a, Y) \approx g(0, Y) + (\theta - a)g'(0, Y) + \tfrac{1}{2}(\theta - a)^2 g''(0, Y).\]
Taking expectations over \(Y\), with \(K_1 = -E_Y[g(0,Y)]\), \(K_2 = -E_Y[g'(0,Y)]\), \(K_3 = -\tfrac{1}{2}E_Y[g''(0,Y)]\): \[L(\theta, a) = -E_Y[g(\theta - a, Y)] \approx K_1 + K_2(\theta - a) + K_3(\theta - a)^2.\]
Completing the square (assuming \(K_3 > 0\)): \[L(\theta, a) \approx K_3\!\left(\theta - a + \frac{K_2}{2K_3}\right)^{\!2} + \text{const}.\]
When \(K_2 = 0\) (symmetric utility in \(y\), symmetric \(Y\)), this is squared-error loss. When \(K_2 \neq 0\), it is shifted squared-error: \((\theta - a + c)^2\), which reduces to standard squared-error by redefining \(\mathcal{A}\).

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Variants of squared-error loss
Weighted squared-error: \(L(\theta, a) = w(\theta)(\theta - a)^2\).
The weight \(w(\theta) > 0\) encodes that a given error may matter more at some parameter values than others.
Example: estimating a Poisson rate \(\theta > 0\). An error of 1 when \(\theta = 0.5\) is much worse than the same error when \(\theta = 100\). The weight \(w(\theta) = 1/\theta^2\) normalizes for scale, giving \(L(\theta, a) = (1 - a/\theta)^2\).
Quadratic loss (multivariate): \(\theta, a \in \mathbb{R}^p\), \(Q\) positive definite: \[L(\theta, a) = (\theta - a)^T Q (\theta - a).\] When \(Q = I_p\): \(L(\theta, a) = \|\theta - a\|^2 = \sum_{i=1}^p (\theta_i - a_i)^2\).
We will encounter this extensively when studying Stein estimation in Week 6.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Example: risk under sum-of-squares loss
\(X = (X_1, \ldots, X_p)^T\) with \(X_i \overset{\text{ind}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\theta_i, 1)\). Estimate \(\theta = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_p)^T\) under \(L(\theta, a) = \|\theta - a\|^2\).
The natural estimator is \(\delta(X) = X\). Its risk: \[R(\theta, \delta) = E_{P_\theta}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^p (\theta_i - X_i)^2\right] = \sum_{i=1}^p \mathrm{Var}_{P_\theta}(X_i) = p.\]
Constant risk \(p\), for all \(\theta\). Seems hard to improve.
For \(p \geq 3\), James and Stein (1960) showed the estimator \[\delta^{JS}(X) = \left(1 - \frac{p-2}{\|X\|^2}\right)X\] satisfies \(R(\theta, \delta^{JS}) < p\) for all \(\theta\). The sample mean vector is inadmissible.
That result is one of the major topics later in the course. For now, it illustrates that the choice of loss (here, sum-of-squares) can have dramatic consequences for which estimators are admissible.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
II. Linear loss
When the utility function is approximately linear over the relevant range, the loss is approximately linear: \[L(\theta, a) = \begin{cases} K_0(\theta - a) & \text{if } \theta - a \geq 0 \quad \text{(underestimation)} \\ K_1(a - \theta) & \text{if } \theta - a < 0 \quad \text{(overestimation)} \end{cases}\]
\(K_0\) and \(K_1\) encode the relative cost of under- vs. overestimation.
When \(K_0 = K_1\): absolute error loss \(L(\theta, a) = |\theta - a|\).
Recall Lecture 1, Example 1 (drug company): \(L(\theta_2, a) = (\theta_2 - a)\) if \(\theta_2 \geq a\) and \(2(a - \theta_2)\) if \(\theta_2 < a\). That is linear loss with \(K_0 = 1\), \(K_1 = 2\): overestimating the adverse reaction rate costs twice as much as underestimating.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: Example: car production under linear loss (Berger, Ex. 17)
text:
Example: car production under linear loss (Berger, Ex. 17)
A company must decide how many cars \(a\) to produce. The demand is \(\theta\) (unknown). Each car sold yields $500 profit. Each unsold car yields $1000 loss.
If \(a \leq \theta\): all cars sell, profit = \(500a\). If \(a > \theta\): \(\theta\) cars sell, \(a - \theta\) do not, profit = \(500\theta - 1000(a - \theta) = 1500\theta - 1000a\).
The regret loss (loss relative to the best action if you knew \(\theta\)): the best action is \(a^* = \theta\) (produce exactly the demand, profit \(500\theta\)). So: \[\ell(\theta, a) = 500\theta - \text{profit}(a) = \begin{cases} 500(\theta - a) & \text{if } a \leq \theta \\ 1500(a - \theta) & \text{if } a > \theta \end{cases}\]
This is linear loss with \(K_0 = 500\) (opportunity cost of underproduction) and \(K_1 = 1500\) (cost of overproduction). The asymmetry \(K_1/K_0 = 3\) reflects that unsold cars are much worse than unmet demand.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
III. “0–1” loss
In a two-action problem (testing \(H_0: \theta \in \Theta_0\) vs. \(H_1: \theta \in \Theta_1\)): \[L(\theta, a_i) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \theta \in \Theta_i \quad \text{(correct)} \\ 1 & \text{if } \theta \in \Theta_j,\ j \neq i \quad \text{(incorrect)} \end{cases}\]
The risk function:

When \(\theta \in \Theta_0\): \(R(\theta, \delta) = P_{P_\theta}(\text{reject } H_0)\) = Type I error.
When \(\theta \in \Theta_1\): \(R(\theta, \delta) = P_{P_\theta}(\text{accept } H_0)\) = Type II error.

The Bayesian expected loss: \(\rho(\pi^*, a_i) = 1 - \pi^*(\theta \in \Theta_i)\).
The error probabilities from Neyman–Pearson theory are the risk function under 0–1 loss. Testing is a special case of decision theory.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: Example: 0–1 loss for a normal test
text:
Example: 0–1 loss for a normal test
\(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, 1)\). Test \(H_0: \theta \leq 0\) vs. \(H_1: \theta > 0\). Decision rule: reject \(H_0\) if \(X > c\).
Under 0–1 loss, the risk function is: \[R(\theta, \delta_c) = \begin{cases}
P_{P_\theta}(X > c) = 1 - \Phi(c - \theta) & \text{if } \theta \leq 0 \quad \text{(Type I)} \\
P_{P_\theta}(X \leq c) = \Phi(c - \theta) & \text{if } \theta > 0 \quad \text{(Type II)}
\end{cases}\]
For \(c = 0\): Type I error at \(\theta = 0\) is \(1 - \Phi(0) = 0.5\). Type II error at \(\theta = 1\) is \(\Phi(-1) = 0.159\).
For \(c = 1.645\): Type I error at \(\theta = 0\) is \(1 - \Phi(1.645) = 0.05\). Type II error at \(\theta = 1\) is \(\Phi(0.645) = 0.74\).
The familiar tradeoff between Type I and Type II error is the tradeoff between the risk function at different values of \(\theta\): making \(R(\theta, \delta_c)\) smaller for \(\theta \leq 0\) makes it larger for \(\theta > 0\), and vice versa.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Weighted 0–1 loss
Basic 0–1 loss treats the two types of error as equally costly. Rarely appropriate.
More realistic: \[L(\theta, a_i) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if correct} \\ K_0 & \text{if reject } H_0 \text{ when } \theta \in \Theta_0 \quad \text{(Type I)} \\ K_1 & \text{if accept } H_0 \text{ when } \theta \in \Theta_1 \quad \text{(Type II)} \end{cases}\]
The risk becomes \(K_0 \cdot P_{P_\theta}(\text{reject } H_0)\) for \(\theta \in \Theta_0\) and \(K_1 \cdot P_{P_\theta}(\text{accept } H_0)\) for \(\theta \in \Theta_1\).
Even more generally: \(L(\theta, a_1) = K_0(\theta)\) for \(\theta \in \Theta_0\), where the weight varies within \(\Theta_0\). A Type I error may be worse when \(\theta\) is far from the boundary.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Inference as a decision problem
The most common use: representing standard inference measures as a risk or Bayesian expected loss.
Confidence sets. Let \(C(x) \subseteq \Theta\) be a confidence rule. The loss \[L(\theta, C(x)) = 1 - I_{C(x)}(\theta) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \theta \in C(x) \\ 1 & \text{if } \theta \notin C(x) \end{cases}\] gives \(R(\theta, C) = 1 - P_{P_\theta}(C(X) \ni \theta)\): one minus the coverage probability.
Unbiased estimation. Restrict to \(E_{P_\theta}[\delta(X)] = \theta\) for all \(\theta\). Under squared-error loss, \(R(\theta, \delta) = \mathrm{Var}_{P_\theta}(\delta(X))\). The UMVUE is the decision-theoretic optimum within this restricted class.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: Scoring rules: quantifying how well confidence works (Berger, Ex. 4)
text:
Scoring rules: quantifying how well confidence works (Berger, Ex. 4)
A confidence rule \(C(x)\) with associated “confidence” \(\alpha(x)\): after observing \(x\), we report \(\alpha(x)\) as our confidence that \(\theta \in C(x)\). How well does \(\alpha(x)\) reflect reality?
The Brier scoring rule: \[L_C(\theta, \alpha(x)) = (\alpha(x) - I_{C(x)}(\theta))^2.\]
When \(\theta \in C(x)\): loss = \((\alpha(x) - 1)^2\), small if \(\alpha(x) \approx 1\).
When \(\theta \notin C(x)\): loss = \(\alpha(x)^2\), small if \(\alpha(x) \approx 0\).

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: Scoring rules continued: constant vs. data-dependent confidence
text:
Scoring rules continued: constant vs. data-dependent confidence
Consider two choices: \(\alpha_1(x) \equiv 0.75\) (constant frequentist confidence) and \(\alpha_2(x) = P(\theta \in C(x) \mid x)\) (posterior probability, a data-dependent confidence).
The risk of \(\alpha_1\) under the Brier loss: \[R(\theta, \alpha_1) = E_{P_\theta}\!\left[(\alpha_1(X) - I_{C(X)}(\theta))^2\right] = 0.75^2 \cdot P_{P_\theta}(\theta \notin C(X)) + 0.25^2 \cdot P_{P_\theta}(\theta \in C(X)).\]
If \(C\) has 75% coverage: \(R(\theta, \alpha_1) = (0.5625)(0.25) + (0.0625)(0.75) = 0.1875\).
The data-dependent confidence \(\alpha_2\) adapts: it reports high confidence when the data are informative and low confidence when they are not. One can show (and we will, in Chapter 4) that \(\alpha_2\) has \(R(\theta, \alpha_2) \leq R(\theta, \alpha_1)\) for all \(\theta\), with strict inequality for some \(\theta\).
The data-dependent confidence dominates the constant one: it communicates better what \(C(x)\) is worth for the specific \(x\) you observed.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Predictive problems
Predict a future observation \(Z\) with density \(g(z|\theta)\), given data \(X\) with density \(f(x|\theta)\).
If the original loss is \(L^*(z, a)\), the decision-theoretic reduction is: \[L(\theta, a) = E_{P_\theta}[L^*(Z, a)] = \int L^*(z, a)\, g(z|\theta)\, dz.\]
This reduces the prediction problem to a standard decision problem involving \(\theta\).

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Example: predicting a normal (Berger, Ex. 5)
\(Z \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\), \(L^*(z, a) = (z - a)^2\). Then: \[\begin{aligned}
L(\theta, a) &= E_{P_\theta}[(Z - a)^2] = E_{P_\theta}[(Z - \theta)^2 + 2(Z - \theta)(\theta - a) + (\theta - a)^2] \\
&= \mathrm{Var}_{P_\theta}(Z) + 0 + (\theta - a)^2 = \sigma^2 + (\theta - a)^2.
\end{aligned}\]
The cross term vanishes because \(E_{P_\theta}[Z - \theta] = 0\).
The constant \(\sigma^2\) does not affect the choice of \(a\). Choosing the best prediction of \(Z\) is equivalent to estimating \(\theta\) under squared-error loss.
This partially explains why decision theorists focus on estimating unknown parameters rather than predicting future observations: prediction problems often reduce to parameter estimation through the loss.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Three criticisms
1. Inappropriate for inference. Many statisticians argue that inference problems do not have a natural loss. A physicist measuring the speed of light cannot know the losses of future users. Decision theorists can accept this and limit their domain, or argue that inference losses (scoring rules, coverage loss) still help even in pure inference settings.
2. Difficult to construct. Utility analysis is laborious. In practice, “standard” losses are used as surrogates.
3. Can be manipulated. Through a biased choice of prior and loss, a statistician can reach any conclusion. “Just make the other conclusions too costly, or too unlikely according to the prior.”

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
The response: robustness
Sensitivity analysis. Show that reasonable variations in the loss do not markedly affect the conclusion. If they do, that signals the need for more careful thought – not the failure of the framework.
Example of sensitivity to loss. Under squared-error loss, the Bayes estimator is the posterior mean. Under absolute error loss, it is the posterior median. Under asymmetric linear loss with \(K_0/(K_0 + K_1) = q\), it is the \(q\)th posterior quantile. If the posterior is symmetric and unimodal, all three agree. If the posterior is skewed, they differ substantially. In that case, the choice of loss matters, and you need to think carefully about whether overestimation and underestimation are equally bad.
“We cannot believe that careful consideration of consequences and losses could be a detriment to statistical analysis.” (Berger)

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
The big picture from today
Utility theory (Berger § 2.1–2.2): Axioms of rational behavior guarantee the existence of a utility function. The loss function is its negative. This justifies expected loss as the decision criterion.
From utility to loss (Berger § 2.4.1): \(L(\theta, a) = -U(\theta, a)\). Random consequences: \(U(\theta, a) = E_{\theta,a}[U(Z)]\). Invariance under affine transformations. Regret loss.
Standard losses (Berger § 2.4.2):

Squared-error: \((\theta - a)^2\). Taylor expansion defense. Weighted, quadratic variants.
Linear: \(K_0, K_1\) encode asymmetry. Car production example.
0–1: risk = error probabilities. Weighted version.

Inference and prediction (Berger § 2.4.3–2.4.4): confidence sets, scoring rules, UMVUE, prediction reduced to estimation.
With this, we have completed the foundations (Berger Ch 1–2). Next: Bayesian decision theory (Berger § 3.3, 4.1–4.4, 4.8).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
In Lectures 1–2 we built the decision-theoretic framework: \(\Theta\), \(\mathcal{A}\), \(L(\theta, a)\), \(\delta\), \(R(\theta, \delta)\), \(\rho(\pi, a)\), \(r(\pi, \delta)\), admissibility, three selection principles, randomized rules. We proved three structural results: sufficiency reduces the problem (Theorem 1), convex loss eliminates randomization (Theorem 3), and the Rao–Blackwell theorem combines them.
In Lecture 3 we grounded the framework: utility theory justifies the loss function and expected loss as the aggregation criterion.
Today we address two topics. First, how to construct priors when no prior information is available (Berger, Section 3.3). Second, the mechanics of Bayesian decision theory: how a prior and a loss function together determine an optimal action (Berger, Sections 4.1–4.4).
Reading: Berger, Section 3.3; Sections 4.1–4.4.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
In Lectures 1–2 we built the decision-theoretic framework: \(\Theta\), \(\mathcal{A}\), \(L(\theta, a)\), \(\delta\), \(R(\theta, \delta)\), \(\rho(\pi, a)\), \(r(\pi, \delta)\), admissibility, three selection principles, randomized rules. We proved three structural results: sufficiency reduces the problem (Theorem 1), convex loss eliminates randomization (Theorem 3), and the Rao–Blackwell theorem combines them.
In Lecture 3 we grounded the framework: utility theory justifies the loss function and expected loss as the aggregation criterion.
Today we address two topics. First, how to construct priors when no prior information is available (Berger, Section 3.3). Second, the mechanics of Bayesian decision theory: how a prior and a loss function together determine an optimal action (Berger, Sections 4.1–4.4).
Reading: Berger, Section 3.3; Sections 4.1–4.4.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
2.1 Motivation
In many decision problems there is no natural prior distribution for \(\theta\). The experimenter may have little prior information, or may wish to report an analysis that does not depend on subjective inputs.
A noninformative prior is intended to represent a state of prior ignorance and let the data speak for themselves. We need these priors because they appear throughout the rest of the course:

As candidates for least favorable priors in minimax theory (Chapter 5).
As the natural priors in invariant decision problems (Chapter 6).
As the object of admissibility analysis for generalized Bayes rules (Chapter 8).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
section: 2.2 Two problems with the naive approach
text:
2.2 Two problems with the naive approach
The first idea is to set \(\pi(\theta) = \text{constant}\). This fails immediately.
Problem 1: Parameterization dependence. Suppose \(\theta \in \mathbb{R}\) and we set \(\pi(\theta) = 1\). Reparameterize: \(\eta = e^\theta\). The induced prior on \(\eta\) is \[
\pi^*(\eta) = \pi(\log \eta) \cdot \left|\frac{d\theta}{d\eta}\right| = \frac{1}{\eta},
\] which is not constant. So “constant” depends on the parameterization.
Problem 2: Impropriety. On \(\mathbb{R}\), a constant function does not integrate to a finite value. It is not a proper probability density.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
3.1 Location parameters (Berger, Example 5)
Suppose \(\mathcal{X}= \Theta = \mathbb{R}^p\) and the density of \(X\) has the form \[
f(x \mid \theta) = f(x - \theta),
\] depending on the data only through \(x - \theta\). Such a density is a location density, and \(\theta\) is a location parameter.
Standard examples: \(\mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\) with \(\sigma^2\) known; \(t(\alpha, \theta, \sigma^2)\) with \(\alpha, \sigma^2\) known; \(\text{Uniform}(\theta - 1, \theta + 1)\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
3.2 Derivation via invariance
Instead of observing \(X\), imagine observing \(Y = X + c\) for some fixed \(c \in \mathbb{R}^p\). Define \(\mu = \theta + c\). Then \(Y\) has density \(f(y - \mu)\), the same functional form as the original problem. The \((X, \theta)\) and \((Y, \mu)\) problems are structurally identical – they differ only by a shift in the origin of measurement.
If two problems are structurally identical, their noninformative priors should agree. Let \(\pi\) and \(\pi^*\) denote the noninformative priors for the \((X, \theta)\) and \((Y, \mu)\) problems. The structural identity argument says \(\pi = \pi^*\): \[
P_\pi(\theta \in A) = P_{\pi^*}(\mu \in A)
\] for any measurable set \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^p\). Since \(\mu = \theta + c\), a change of variables gives \(P_{\pi^*}(\mu \in A) = P_\pi(\theta \in A - c)\). Combining: \[
P_\pi(\theta \in A) = P_\pi(\theta \in A - c) \quad \text{for all } c \in \mathbb{R}^p.
\]
Any prior satisfying this is called location invariant. In terms of densities: \[
\int_A \pi(\theta)\, d\theta = \int_A \pi(\theta - c)\, d\theta \quad \text{for all } A.
\] This forces \(\pi(\theta) = \pi(\theta - c)\) for all \(\theta, c\), so \(\pi\) is constant.

The noninformative prior for a location parameter is \(\pi(\theta) = 1\).

This is improper on \(\mathbb{R}^p\): \(\int_{\mathbb{R}^p} 1\, d\theta = \infty\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
3.3 The non-uniqueness issue
The argument above required that structurally identical problems have the same noninformative prior. But an improper prior \(\pi\) and \(K\pi\) (for any \(K > 0\)) produce identical posteriors and Bayes actions. Relaxing exact equality to proportionality gives the weaker condition \[
P_\pi(\theta \in A) = h(c)\, P_\pi(\theta \in A - c)
\] for some positive function \(h\). This leads to the functional equation \[
\pi(\theta) = h(c)\, \pi(\theta - c).
\] Setting \(\theta = c\): \(h(c) = \pi(c)/\pi(0)\), so \[
\pi(\theta - c) = \frac{\pi(\theta)\, \pi(0)}{\pi(c)}.
\]
Any prior satisfying this is relatively location invariant. The constant prior satisfies it, but so does \(\pi(\theta) = \exp(\theta' z)\) for any fixed vector \(z\). There are many relatively location invariant priors.
For certain statistical problems, a natural selection from this class is the right invariant Haar measure on the relevant transformation group. The priors derived in this section are the right invariant Haar measures for the location and scale groups. We return to this in Chapter 6.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
3.4 Scale parameters (Berger, Example 6)
A scale density has the form \[
f(x \mid \sigma) = \sigma^{-1} g(x/\sigma),
\] where \(\sigma > 0\). Standard examples: \(\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\); \(\text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)\) with \(\alpha\) fixed; \(\text{Exponential}(\sigma)\).
Derivation. Observe \(Y = cX\) for some \(c > 0\) and define \(\eta = c\sigma\). Then \(Y\) has density \(\eta^{-1}g(y/\eta)\), structurally identical to the original. The invariance argument gives: \(\pi\) should satisfy \[
P_\pi(\sigma \in A) = P_\pi(\sigma \in c^{-1}A)
\] for all \(c > 0\) and measurable \(A \subseteq (0, \infty)\). In terms of densities: \[
\pi(\sigma) = c^{-1}\pi(c^{-1}\sigma) \quad \text{for all } \sigma, c.
\] Setting \(\sigma = c\): \(\pi(c) = c^{-1}\pi(1)\). With \(\pi(1) = 1\):

The noninformative prior for a scale parameter is \(\pi(\sigma) = \sigma^{-1}\).

Again improper: \(\int_0^\infty \sigma^{-1}\, d\sigma = \infty\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
3.5 Benford’s law (Berger, Example 6 continued)
An application of the scale-invariant prior. The distribution of first significant digits in tables of physical constants, population sizes, etc., follows Benford’s law: digit \(i\) occurs with relative frequency \(\log(1 + i^{-1})/\log 10\).
If table entries have a scale-invariant distribution \(\pi(u) = u^{-1}\), then normalizing on \((1, 10)\) gives \(\pi(u) = u^{-1}/\log 10\), and \[
P(\text{first digit} = i) = \int_i^{i+1} \frac{u^{-1}}{\log 10}\, du = \frac{\log(1 + i^{-1})}{\log 10},
\] matching the empirical observation. Furthermore, as the interval \((a, b)\) expands to \((0, \infty)\), these probabilities converge to the same values regardless of the choice of \(a\) and \(b\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
3.6 Location-scale problems (Berger, Example 7)
When the density has the form \(\sigma^{-1}f\bigl((x - \theta)/\sigma\bigr)\) with both \(\theta \in \mathbb{R}\) and \(\sigma > 0\) unknown, two approaches compete.
Approach 1 (Jeffreys’ rule). The Fisher information matrix for \(\mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\) is \[
\mathcal{I}(\theta, \sigma) = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma^{-2} & 0 \\ 0 & 2\sigma^{-2} \end{pmatrix}.
\] Jeffreys’ rule (next section) gives \(\pi(\theta, \sigma) = [\det \mathcal{I}(\theta, \sigma)]^{1/2} \propto \sigma^{-2}\).
Approach 2 (Independence). Assume \(\theta\) and \(\sigma\) are a priori independent. Multiply marginal noninformative priors: \(\pi(\theta, \sigma) = 1 \cdot \sigma^{-1} = \sigma^{-1}\).
These disagree (\(\sigma^{-2}\) vs. \(\sigma^{-1}\)). Jeffreys himself ultimately recommended \(\sigma^{-1}\) for location-scale problems, arguing that \([\det \mathcal{I}]^{1/2}\) sometimes gives inferior results in multiparameter settings. The prior \(\sigma^{-1}\) is also the right invariant Haar density for the location-scale group.

Convention. For location-scale problems, use \(\pi(\theta, \sigma) = \sigma^{-1}\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
4.1 Definition: scalar case
For a general one-dimensional parameter \(\theta\), Jeffreys (1961) proposed \[
\pi(\theta) = [I(\theta)]^{1/2},
\] where \(I(\theta)\) is the expected Fisher information: \[
I(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{P_\theta}\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \log f(X \mid \theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right].
\]

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
4.2 Reparameterization invariance
Under \(\eta = h(\theta)\), the Fisher information transforms as \(I_\eta(\eta) = I_\theta(\theta)/[h'(\theta)]^2\). The Jeffreys prior transforms as \[
\pi_\eta(\eta) = [I_\eta(\eta)]^{1/2} = \frac{[I_\theta(\theta)]^{1/2}}{|h'(\theta)|}.
\] The change-of-variables formula gives \(\pi_\eta(\eta) = \pi_\theta(\theta)/|h'(\theta)|\), the same expression. So the Jeffreys prior is invariant under reparameterization: compute it in one parameterization and transform, or compute it directly in the new parameterization – the answer is the same.
This is the property that distinguishes the Jeffreys prior from the naive constant prior.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
4.3 Definition: vector case
For \(\theta = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_p)'\): \[
\pi(\theta) = [\det \mathcal{I}(\theta)]^{1/2},
\] where \(\mathcal{I}(\theta)\) is the \(p \times p\) Fisher information matrix with \((i,j)\) entry \[
\mathcal{I}_{ij}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{P_\theta}\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \log f(X \mid \theta)}{\partial \theta_i\, \partial \theta_j}\right].
\]

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
4.4 Verification for location and scale
Location. If \(f(x \mid \theta) = f(x - \theta)\), then \(\frac{\partial^2}{\partial \theta^2}\log f(x \mid \theta)\) depends on \(x - \theta\). The expectation of this under \(P_\theta\) does not depend on \(\theta\) (substitute \(z = x - \theta\)). So \(I(\theta)\) is constant, and \(\pi(\theta) \propto 1\), recovering the location-invariance result.
Scale. If \(f(x \mid \sigma) = \sigma^{-1}g(x/\sigma)\), a computation shows \(I(\sigma) \propto \sigma^{-2}\), giving \(\pi(\sigma) \propto \sigma^{-1}\), recovering the scale-invariance result.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
4.5 Board examples
Bernoulli. \(X \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\theta)\), \(\theta \in (0,1)\). Then \[
-\frac{\partial^2}{\partial \theta^2}\log f(x \mid \theta) = \frac{x}{\theta^2} + \frac{1-x}{(1-\theta)^2}.
\] Taking expectations: \(I(\theta) = \theta^{-1} + (1-\theta)^{-1} = [\theta(1-\theta)]^{-1}\). The Jeffreys prior is \[
\pi(\theta) = [\theta(1-\theta)]^{-1/2},
\] the density of a \(\text{Beta}(1/2, 1/2)\) distribution. This is a proper prior.
Poisson. \(X \sim \text{Poisson}(\theta)\), \(\theta > 0\). Then \(I(\theta) = \theta^{-1}\), so \(\pi(\theta) = \theta^{-1/2}\). Improper.
Normal mean. \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\), \(\sigma^2\) known. Then \(I(\theta) = \sigma^{-2}\), so \(\pi(\theta) = \sigma^{-1} \propto 1\) (constant). Improper.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
section: 4.6 The multiplicity of noninformative priors (Berger, Example 8)
text:
4.6 The multiplicity of noninformative priors (Berger, Example 8)
For the binomial parameter \(\theta \in [0,1]\), several noninformative priors have been proposed:








Prior
Source
Formula




\(\pi_1(\theta) = 1\)
Bayes (1763), Laplace (1812)
\(\text{Beta}(1,1)\)


\(\pi_2(\theta) = [\theta(1-\theta)]^{-1}\)
Novick–Hall, Jaynes, Villegas
Improper (Haldane)


\(\pi_3(\theta) \propto [\theta(1-\theta)]^{-1/2}\)
Jeffreys, Box–Tiao, Bernardo
\(\text{Beta}(1/2, 1/2)\)


\(\pi_4(\theta) \propto \theta^{1/1}(1-\theta)^{1-1/1}\)
Zellner
context-dependent



All four are defensible. Whether this multiplicity matters depends on sample size: for large \(n\), the four posteriors are nearly identical. For small \(n\), they can differ, but for small \(n\) one typically has real prior information that should be used.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
4.7 Criticisms and caveats (Berger $$3.3.4)
Three points worth noting.
1. Violation of the Likelihood Principle. The Jeffreys prior depends on the expected Fisher information, which depends on the sampling model, not just the likelihood. Different experimental designs with the same likelihood can lead to different Jeffreys priors.
2. The marginalization paradox (Dawid, Stone, and Zidek, 1973). In certain multiparameter problems, marginalizing over a nuisance parameter under an improper prior can give results that depend on the order of integration.
3. Poor performance in high dimensions. The noninformative prior \(\pi(\theta) = 1\) for \(\theta \in \mathbb{R}^p\) implicitly assigns increasing prior weight to large \(\|\theta\|^2\) as \(p\) grows. The resulting generalized Bayes estimator \(\delta^0(x) = x\) is inadmissible for \(p \geq 3\) (James and Stein, 1960). This is not an indictment of the Bayesian machinery; it is an indictment of the specific prior.
Convention (restricted parameter spaces). If it is known that \(\theta > 0\) in a location problem, the noninformative prior is still \(\pi(\theta) = 1\), restricted to \((0, \infty)\). The prior is inherited from the unrestricted parameter space.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
section: 5.1 Why this section matters for decision theory (Berger $$4.1)
text:
5.1 Why this section matters for decision theory (Berger $$4.1)
The posterior distribution \(\pi(\theta \mid x)\) is the key computational object in Bayesian decision theory. Once we have it, choosing the optimal action reduces to a one-dimensional minimization problem (Section 4.4). The posterior is not the goal; it is the intermediate step.
For second-year students: the mechanics of computing posteriors and conjugate families are review from 220A. The new content here is the treatment of improper priors (Section 4.2.3), which connects directly to the noninformative priors we just derived.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
5.2 Bayes’ theorem (Berger $$4.2.1)
For a proper prior \(\pi(\theta)\): \[
\pi(\theta \mid x) = \frac{f(x \mid \theta)\, \pi(\theta)}{m(x)}, \qquad m(x) = \int_\Theta f(x \mid \theta)\, \pi(\theta)\, d\theta.
\]
The marginal \(m(x)\) is a normalizing constant. We often write \(\pi(\theta \mid x) \propto f(x \mid \theta)\, \pi(\theta)\) and identify the posterior by its kernel.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
5.3 Conjugate families (Berger $$4.2.2)
A class \(\mathcal{G}\) of prior distributions is conjugate for a class \(\mathcal{F}\) of sampling densities if the posterior \(\pi(\theta \mid x) \in \mathcal{G}\) whenever the prior \(\pi \in \mathcal{G}\) and the sampling density \(f \in \mathcal{F}\).
Running example. \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\), \(\sigma^2\) known. Prior: \(\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2)\). Posterior: \[
\pi(\theta \mid x) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(\frac{\tau^2}{\sigma^2 + \tau^2}\, x + \frac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2 + \tau^2}\, \mu,\; \frac{\sigma^2 \tau^2}{\sigma^2 + \tau^2}\right).
\]
The posterior mean is a weighted average of \(x\) (data) and \(\mu\) (prior mean), with weights proportional to precisions. The posterior variance is smaller than both \(\sigma^2\) and \(\tau^2\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
5.4 Improper priors and formal posteriors (Berger $$4.2.3)
The mechanics of Bayes’ theorem carry over to improper priors without modification. If \(\pi(\theta)\) is improper, define \[
h(x, \theta) = f(x \mid \theta)\, \pi(\theta), \qquad m(x) = \int_\Theta f(x \mid \theta)\, \pi(\theta)\, d\theta,
\] and, whenever \(0 < m(x) < \infty\), \[
\pi(\theta \mid x) = \frac{f(x \mid \theta)\, \pi(\theta)}{\int_\Theta f(x \mid \theta)\, \pi(\theta)\, d\theta}.
\]
The resulting \(\pi(\theta \mid x)\) is the formal posterior. It cannot rigorously be interpreted as a conditional distribution (since \(\pi\) is not a probability measure), but the following heuristic supports the interpretation: if \(\{\pi_n\}\) is a sequence of proper priors converging to \(\pi\), then under regularity conditions \(\pi_n(\theta \mid x) \to \pi(\theta \mid x)\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
5.5 When does this work?
The computation is valid if and only if \(m(x) = \int f(x \mid \theta)\, \pi(\theta)\, d\theta\) is finite and positive for (almost) all \(x\). When it is, \(\pi(\theta \mid x)\) is a proper probability density even though \(\pi(\theta)\) is not.
Normal location – it works. \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\), \(\pi(\theta) = 1\). \[
m(x) = \int_{-\infty}^\infty (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-1/2} \exp\!\left\{-\frac{(x - \theta)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right\} d\theta = 1.
\] The posterior is \(\pi(\theta \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(x, \sigma^2)\). Proper.
This is the running normal example with the conjugate prior \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2)\) taken to its limit: as \(\tau^2 \to \infty\), the posterior mean converges to \(x\) and the posterior variance converges to \(\sigma^2\).
Normal scale – it works. \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\), \(\pi(\sigma) = \sigma^{-1}\), \(n \geq 1\). \[
m(x) = \int_0^\infty \sigma^{-n}(2\pi)^{-n/2} \exp\!\left\{-\frac{\sum x_i^2}{2\sigma^2}\right\}\sigma^{-1}\, d\sigma.
\] The substitution \(u = 1/\sigma^2\) shows this is a Gamma integral, finite when \(n \geq 1\) and \(\sum x_i^2 > 0\). The posterior for \(1/\sigma^2\) is Gamma; equivalently, the posterior for \(\sigma^2\) is Inverse-Gamma. Proper.
A case where it fails. \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \theta^2)\) with \(\theta > 0\) (the mean equals the standard deviation). Consider \(\pi(\theta) = \theta^{-1}\). \[
m(x) = \int_0^\infty \frac{1}{\theta^2\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left\{-\frac{(x - \theta)^2}{2\theta^2}\right\} d\theta.
\] The substitution \(u = x/\theta\) transforms this to an integral that diverges. The formal posterior is improper, and the Bayes computation is not valid. This is a case where the “natural” noninformative prior fails.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
6.1 Posterior expected loss (Berger, Definition 8)
The posterior expected loss of an action \(a\), when the posterior is \(\pi(\theta \mid x)\), is \[
\rho(\pi(\theta \mid x),\, a) = \int_\Theta L(\theta, a)\, dF^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}(\theta).
\]
A (posterior) Bayes action \(\delta^\pi(x)\) is any action \(a \in \mathcal{A}\) that minimizes \(\rho(\pi(\theta \mid x), a)\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
section: 6.2 Normal form equals extensive form (Berger, Result 1)
text:
6.2 Normal form equals extensive form (Berger, Result 1)
Result 1. A Bayes rule \(\delta^\pi\) (i.e., a rule minimizing \(r(\pi, \delta)\)) can be found by choosing, for each \(x\) with \(m(x) > 0\), an action that minimizes the posterior expected loss. The rule can be defined arbitrarily when \(m(x) = 0\).
Proof. By Fubini’s theorem (applicable since \(L(\theta, a) \geq -K > -\infty\) and all measures are finite): \[
r(\pi, \delta) = \int_\Theta R(\theta, \delta)\, dF^\pi(\theta) = \int_{\{x:\, m(x)>0\}} \rho(\pi(\theta \mid x),\, \delta(x))\, dm(x).
\] A minimizing \(\delta\) can be found by minimizing the integrand for each \(x\). \(\square\)
This is the link between the normal form (minimizing \(r(\pi, \delta)\) over all decision rules) and the extensive form (minimizing posterior expected loss pointwise in \(x\)). They give the same answer. We will virtually always work in the extensive form.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
6.3 Generalized Bayes rules (Berger, Definition 9)
If \(\pi\) is an improper prior but \(\delta^\pi(x)\) minimizes the posterior expected loss for each \(x\) with \(m(x) > 0\), then \(\delta^\pi\) is called a generalized Bayes rule.
Generalized Bayes rules inherit the computational machinery of Bayesian decision theory. Their decision-theoretic properties (admissibility, minimaxity) are more delicate. We study this in the next lecture (Section 4.8).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
section: 6.4 Bayes estimators under squared-error loss (Berger, Result 3)
text:
6.4 Bayes estimators under squared-error loss (Berger, Result 3)
Result 3. If \(L(\theta, a) = (\theta - a)^2\), the Bayes rule is \[
\delta^\pi(x) = \mathbb{E}^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}[\theta],
\] the mean of the posterior distribution.
Proof. The posterior expected loss is \(\mathbb{E}^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}[(\theta - a)^2]\). Differentiate with respect to \(a\) and set to zero: \[
\frac{d}{da}\mathbb{E}^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}[(\theta - a)^2] = -2\,\mathbb{E}^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}[\theta] + 2a = 0,
\] so \(a = \mathbb{E}^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}[\theta]\). \(\square\)
Running example. \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\), prior \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2)\). The posterior mean is \[
\delta^\pi(x) = \frac{\tau^2}{\sigma^2 + \tau^2}\, x + \frac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2 + \tau^2}\, \mu.
\] A weighted average, shrinking \(x\) toward \(\mu\). The amount of shrinkage depends on \(\tau^2/\sigma^2\): the more spread the prior, the less shrinkage.
Under the noninformative prior \(\pi(\theta) = 1\): the formal posterior is \(\mathcal{N}(x, \sigma^2)\), and the generalized Bayes estimator is \(\delta^0(x) = x\). The sample mean. The noninformative prior recovers it automatically.
Quadratic loss (multivariate). For \(L(\theta, a) = (\theta - a)'Q(\theta - a)\) with \(Q\) positive definite, the Bayes estimator is still the posterior mean. The matrix \(Q\) has no effect on the point estimate.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
section: 6.5 Bayes estimators under absolute-error loss (Berger, Result 5)
text:
6.5 Bayes estimators under absolute-error loss (Berger, Result 5)
Result 5. If \(L(\theta, a) = |\theta - a|\), any median of \(\pi(\theta \mid x)\) is a Bayes estimator.
Proof. Let \(m\) denote a median and let \(a > m\). Then \[
L(\theta, m) - L(\theta, a) \leq (m - a)\,\mathbf{1}_{(-\infty, m]}(\theta) + (a - m)\,\mathbf{1}_{(m, \infty)}(\theta).
\] Taking posterior expectations: \[
\mathbb{E}^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}[L(\theta, m) - L(\theta, a)] \leq (m - a)\,P(\theta \leq m \mid x) + (a - m)\,P(\theta > m \mid x).
\] Since \(m\) is a median, \(P(\theta \leq m \mid x) \geq 1/2\) and \(P(\theta > m \mid x) \leq 1/2\). Since \(m - a < 0\) and \(a - m > 0\): \[
\leq (m-a)\cdot\tfrac{1}{2} + (a-m)\cdot\tfrac{1}{2} = 0.
\] So \(m\) has posterior expected loss at most that of \(a\). A symmetric argument handles \(a < m\). \(\square\)
Running example. The \(\mathcal{N}(\mu(x), \sigma^2\tau^2/(\sigma^2 + \tau^2))\) posterior is symmetric, so the posterior mean and posterior median coincide. The Bayes estimator is the same under both losses.
More generally: when the posterior is unimodal and symmetric, the Bayes estimator under any loss of the form \(L(|\theta - a|)\) with \(L\) increasing is the posterior median (which equals the posterior mean). The specific shape of \(L\) does not matter much, as long as under- and overestimation are treated symmetrically.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
section: 6.6 Bayes estimators under linear loss (Berger, Result 6)
text:
6.6 Bayes estimators under linear loss (Berger, Result 6)
Result 6. If \[
L(\theta, a) = \begin{cases} K_0(\theta - a) & \text{if } \theta - a \geq 0 \\ K_1(a - \theta) & \text{if } \theta - a < 0, \end{cases}
\] any \(\bigl(K_0/(K_0 + K_1)\bigr)\)-fractile of \(\pi(\theta \mid x)\) is a Bayes estimator.
When \(K_0 = K_1\): the \(1/2\)-fractile is the median, recovering Result 5.
When \(K_0 > K_1\) (underestimation is worse): the Bayes estimator is a fractile above the median – the estimator shifts upward to protect against underestimation.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
6.7 Hypothesis testing under 0–1 loss (Berger $$4.4.3)
Test \(H_0: \theta \in \Theta_0\) vs. \(H_1: \theta \in \Theta_1\). Actions: \(a_0\) (accept \(H_0\)), \(a_1\) (reject \(H_0\)). Under 0–1 loss:
\[
\mathbb{E}^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}[L(\theta, a_1)] = P(\Theta_0 \mid x), \qquad \mathbb{E}^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}[L(\theta, a_0)] = P(\Theta_1 \mid x).
\]
The Bayes action is to accept the hypothesis with the larger posterior probability.
Under weighted 0–1 loss (\(K_0\) for Type I, \(K_1\) for Type II): accept \(H_0\) if \(K_0\, P(\Theta_0 \mid x) \leq K_1\, P(\Theta_1 \mid x)\), equivalently if the posterior odds \(P(\Theta_1 \mid x)/P(\Theta_0 \mid x)\) exceed \(K_0/K_1\).
