title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Recap
In Lectures 1–2 we built the decision-theoretic framework: \(\Theta\), \(\mathcal{A}\), \(L(\theta, a)\), \(\delta\), \(R(\theta, \delta)\), \(\rho(\pi, a)\), \(r(\pi, \delta)\), admissibility, three selection principles, randomized rules.
We proved three structural results: sufficiency reduces the problem (Theorem 1), convex loss eliminates randomization (Theorem 3), and the Rao–Blackwell theorem combines them.
In Lecture 3 we grounded the framework: utility theory justifies the loss function and expected loss as the aggregation criterion.
Today we address two topics:

How to construct priors when no prior information is available (Berger, Section 3.3).
The mechanics of Bayesian decision theory: how a prior and a loss function determine an optimal action (Berger, Sections 4.1–4.4).

Reading: Berger, Section 3.3; Sections 4.1–4.4.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Why noninformative priors? (Berger $$3.3.1)
In many decision problems there is no natural prior distribution for \(\theta\). A noninformative prior is intended to represent prior ignorance and let the data speak for themselves.
We need these priors because they appear throughout the rest of the course:

As candidates for least favorable priors in minimax theory (Chapter 5).
As the natural priors in invariant decision problems (Chapter 6).
As the object of admissibility analysis for generalized Bayes rules (Chapter 8).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
section: Two problems with the naive approach
text:
Two problems with the naive approach
The first idea: set \(\pi(\theta) = \text{constant}\). This fails immediately.
Problem 1: Parameterization dependence. Suppose \(\theta \in \mathbb{R}\) and \(\pi(\theta) = 1\). Reparameterize: \(\eta = e^\theta\). The induced prior on \(\eta\) is \[
\pi^*(\eta) = \pi(\log \eta) \cdot \left|\frac{d\theta}{d\eta}\right| = \frac{1}{\eta},
\] which is not constant. “Constant” depends on the parameterization.
Problem 2: Impropriety. On \(\mathbb{R}\), a constant function does not integrate to a finite value. It is not a proper probability density.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Location parameters: setup (Berger, Example 5)
Suppose \(\mathcal{X}= \Theta = \mathbb{R}^p\) and the density of \(X\) has the form \[
f(x \mid \theta) = f(x - \theta),
\] depending on the data only through \(x - \theta\). Such a density is a location density, and \(\theta\) is a location parameter.
Standard examples:

\(\mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\) with \(\sigma^2\) known
\(t(\alpha, \theta, \sigma^2)\) with \(\alpha, \sigma^2\) known
\(\text{Uniform}(\theta - 1, \theta + 1)\)

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Location parameters: derivation via invariance
Instead of observing \(X\), observe \(Y = X + c\) for fixed \(c \in \mathbb{R}^p\). Define \(\mu = \theta + c\). Then \(Y\) has density \(f(y - \mu)\): structurally identical to the original.
If the problems are structurally identical, they should share the same noninformative prior. Writing \(\pi\) for both: \[
P_\pi(\theta \in A) = P_\pi(\theta \in A - c) \quad \text{for all } c \in \mathbb{R}^p.
\] Any prior satisfying this is location invariant. In terms of densities: \[
\int_A \pi(\theta)\, d\theta = \int_A \pi(\theta - c)\, d\theta \quad \text{for all } A.
\] This forces \(\pi(\theta) = \pi(\theta - c)\) for all \(\theta, c\), so \(\pi\) is constant.

The noninformative prior for a location parameter is \(\pi(\theta) = 1\).

Improper on \(\mathbb{R}^p\): \(\int_{\mathbb{R}^p} 1\, d\theta = \infty\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
The non-uniqueness issue
The argument required \(\pi = \pi^*\) exactly. But \(\pi\) and \(K\pi\) produce identical posteriors. Relaxing to proportionality: \[
\pi(\theta) = h(c)\, \pi(\theta - c)
\] for some positive function \(h\). Setting \(\theta = c\): \(h(c) = \pi(c)/\pi(0)\), giving \[
\pi(\theta - c) = \frac{\pi(\theta)\, \pi(0)}{\pi(c)}.
\]
Any such prior is relatively location invariant. Besides \(\pi(\theta) = 1\), this includes \(\pi(\theta) = \exp(\theta' z)\) for any fixed \(z\).
Selecting among them requires more structure. For certain problems, the natural choice is the right invariant Haar measure on the transformation group. We return to this in Chapter 6.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Scale parameters (Berger, Example 6)
A scale density has the form \(f(x \mid \sigma) = \sigma^{-1} g(x/\sigma)\), \(\sigma > 0\).
Standard examples: \(\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\); \(\text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)\) with \(\alpha\) fixed; \(\text{Exponential}(\sigma)\).
Derivation. Observe \(Y = cX\), \(c > 0\), define \(\eta = c\sigma\). Then \(Y\) has density \(\eta^{-1}g(y/\eta)\): structurally identical. The scale-invariance condition: \[
\pi(\sigma) = c^{-1}\pi(c^{-1}\sigma) \quad \text{for all } \sigma, c.
\] Setting \(\sigma = c\): \(\pi(c) = c^{-1}\pi(1)\).

The noninformative prior for a scale parameter is \(\pi(\sigma) = \sigma^{-1}\).

Improper: \(\int_0^\infty \sigma^{-1}\, d\sigma = \infty\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Benford’s law (Berger, Example 6 continued)
The distribution of first significant digits in tables of physical constants, population sizes, etc., follows Benford’s law: digit \(i\) occurs with relative frequency \(\log(1 + i^{-1})/\log 10\).
If table entries have a scale-invariant distribution \(\pi(u) = u^{-1}\), normalizing on \((1, 10)\): \[
P(\text{first digit} = i) = \int_i^{i+1} \frac{u^{-1}}{\log 10}\, du = \frac{\log(1 + i^{-1})}{\log 10},
\] matching the empirical observation. As \((a, b) \to (0, \infty)\), the probabilities converge to the same values regardless of \(a, b\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Location-scale problems (Berger, Example 7)
Density: \(\sigma^{-1}f\bigl((x - \theta)/\sigma\bigr)\), both \(\theta \in \mathbb{R}\) and \(\sigma > 0\) unknown. Two approaches:
Approach 1 (Jeffreys’ rule). Fisher information for \(\mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\): \[
\mathcal{I}(\theta, \sigma) = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma^{-2} & 0 \\ 0 & 2\sigma^{-2} \end{pmatrix}.
\] Gives \(\pi(\theta, \sigma) = [\det \mathcal{I}(\theta, \sigma)]^{1/2} \propto \sigma^{-2}\).
Approach 2 (Independence). Multiply marginal noninformative priors: \(\pi(\theta, \sigma) = 1 \cdot \sigma^{-1} = \sigma^{-1}\).
These disagree. Jeffreys himself recommended \(\sigma^{-1}\), arguing that \([\det \mathcal{I}]^{1/2}\) sometimes gives inferior results in multiparameter settings. The prior \(\sigma^{-1}\) is also the right invariant Haar density.

Convention. For location-scale problems, use \(\pi(\theta, \sigma) = \sigma^{-1}\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Definition: scalar case
For a general one-dimensional parameter \(\theta\), Jeffreys (1961) proposed \[
\pi(\theta) = [I(\theta)]^{1/2},
\] where \(I(\theta)\) is the expected Fisher information: \[
I(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{P_\theta}\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \log f(X \mid \theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right].
\]

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Reparameterization invariance
Under \(\eta = h(\theta)\), Fisher information transforms as \(I_\eta(\eta) = I_\theta(\theta)/[h'(\theta)]^2\). The Jeffreys prior: \[
\pi_\eta(\eta) = [I_\eta(\eta)]^{1/2} = \frac{[I_\theta(\theta)]^{1/2}}{|h'(\theta)|}.
\] The change-of-variables formula for densities gives the same expression: \(\pi_\eta(\eta) = \pi_\theta(\theta)/|h'(\theta)|\).
So the Jeffreys prior is invariant under reparameterization: compute it in one parameterization and transform, or compute directly in the new parameterization – the answer is the same.
This is the property that distinguishes it from the naive constant prior.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Definition: vector case
For \(\theta = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_p)'\): \[
\pi(\theta) = [\det \mathcal{I}(\theta)]^{1/2},
\] where \(\mathcal{I}(\theta)\) is the \(p \times p\) Fisher information matrix with \((i,j)\) entry \[
\mathcal{I}_{ij}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{P_\theta}\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \log f(X \mid \theta)}{\partial \theta_i\, \partial \theta_j}\right].
\]

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Verification for location and scale
Location. If \(f(x \mid \theta) = f(x - \theta)\), then \(\frac{\partial^2}{\partial \theta^2}\log f(x \mid \theta)\) depends on \(x - \theta\). The expectation under \(P_\theta\) does not depend on \(\theta\) (substitute \(z = x - \theta\)). So \(I(\theta)\) is constant, and \(\pi(\theta) \propto 1\). Recovers the location-invariance result.
Scale. If \(f(x \mid \sigma) = \sigma^{-1}g(x/\sigma)\), a computation shows \(I(\sigma) \propto \sigma^{-2}\), giving \(\pi(\sigma) \propto \sigma^{-1}\). Recovers the scale-invariance result.
The Jeffreys prior unifies both cases under a single formula.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Board examples
Bernoulli. \(X \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\theta)\), \(\theta \in (0,1)\). \[
I(\theta) = \frac{1}{\theta} + \frac{1}{1-\theta} = [\theta(1-\theta)]^{-1}. \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \pi(\theta) = [\theta(1-\theta)]^{-1/2}.
\] This is a \(\text{Beta}(1/2, 1/2)\) density. Proper.
Poisson. \(X \sim \text{Poisson}(\theta)\), \(\theta > 0\). \[
I(\theta) = \theta^{-1}. \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \pi(\theta) = \theta^{-1/2}. \quad \text{Improper.}
\]
Normal mean. \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\), \(\sigma^2\) known. \[
I(\theta) = \sigma^{-2}. \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \pi(\theta) \propto 1. \quad \text{Improper.}
\]

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
section: The multiplicity of noninformative priors (Berger, Example 8)
text:
The multiplicity of noninformative priors (Berger, Example 8)
For the binomial parameter \(\theta \in [0,1]\), several noninformative priors:








Prior
Source
Formula




\(\pi_1(\theta) = 1\)
Bayes, Laplace
\(\text{Beta}(1,1)\)


\(\pi_2(\theta) = [\theta(1-\theta)]^{-1}\)
Novick–Hall, Jaynes
Improper


\(\pi_3(\theta) \propto [\theta(1-\theta)]^{-1/2}\)
Jeffreys, Box–Tiao
\(\text{Beta}(1/2, 1/2)\)



All three are defensible. For large \(n\), the posteriors are nearly identical. For small \(n\), they can differ – but for small \(n\) one typically has real prior information that should be used.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Criticisms and caveats (Berger $$3.3.4)
1. Violation of the Likelihood Principle. The Jeffreys prior depends on the expected Fisher information, which depends on the sampling model, not just the likelihood. Different experimental designs with the same likelihood can produce different Jeffreys priors.
2. The marginalization paradox (Dawid, Stone, and Zidek, 1973). In certain multiparameter problems, marginalizing under an improper prior can give results that depend on the order of integration.
3. Poor performance in high dimensions. The prior \(\pi(\theta) = 1\) for \(\theta \in \mathbb{R}^p\) implicitly assigns increasing weight to large \(\|\theta\|^2\) as \(p\) grows. The resulting generalized Bayes estimator \(\delta^0(x) = x\) is inadmissible for \(p \geq 3\) (James–Stein). Not an indictment of the Bayesian machinery; an indictment of the specific prior.
Convention (restricted parameter spaces). If \(\theta > 0\) in a location problem, the noninformative prior is still \(\pi(\theta) = 1\) on \((0, \infty)\). Inherited from the unrestricted space.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Why this section matters (Berger $$4.1)
The posterior distribution \(\pi(\theta \mid x)\) is the key computational object in Bayesian decision theory. Once we have it, choosing the optimal action reduces to a one-dimensional minimization (Section 4.4).
The posterior is not the goal; it is the intermediate step.
The mechanics of computing posteriors and conjugate families are review from 220A. The new content is the treatment of improper priors (Section 4.2.3), which connects to the noninformative priors we just derived.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Bayes’ theorem and conjugate families (Berger $$4.2.1–4.2.2)
For a proper prior \(\pi(\theta)\): \[
\pi(\theta \mid x) = \frac{f(x \mid \theta)\, \pi(\theta)}{m(x)}, \qquad m(x) = \int_\Theta f(x \mid \theta)\, \pi(\theta)\, d\theta.
\]
A class \(\mathcal{G}\) of priors is conjugate for \(\mathcal{F}\) if the posterior stays in \(\mathcal{G}\).
Running example. \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\), prior \(\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2)\). Posterior: \[
\pi(\theta \mid x) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(\frac{\tau^2}{\sigma^2 + \tau^2}\, x + \frac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2 + \tau^2}\, \mu,\; \frac{\sigma^2 \tau^2}{\sigma^2 + \tau^2}\right).
\]
Posterior mean: weighted average of \(x\) and \(\mu\), weights proportional to precisions. Posterior variance: smaller than both \(\sigma^2\) and \(\tau^2\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Improper priors and formal posteriors (Berger $$4.2.3)
The mechanics carry over. If \(\pi(\theta)\) is improper, define \[
m(x) = \int_\Theta f(x \mid \theta)\, \pi(\theta)\, d\theta,
\] and whenever \(0 < m(x) < \infty\): \[
\pi(\theta \mid x) = \frac{f(x \mid \theta)\, \pi(\theta)}{m(x)}.
\]
This is the formal posterior. Not rigorously a conditional distribution, but justified heuristically: if \(\{\pi_n\}\) is a sequence of proper priors converging to \(\pi\), then \(\pi_n(\theta \mid x) \to \pi(\theta \mid x)\) under regularity conditions.
The computation is valid if and only if \(m(x)\) is finite and positive for (almost) all \(x\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Normal location – it works
\(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\), \(\pi(\theta) = 1\). \[
m(x) = \int_{-\infty}^\infty (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-1/2} \exp\!\left\{-\frac{(x - \theta)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right\} d\theta = 1.
\] Posterior: \(\pi(\theta \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(x, \sigma^2)\). Proper.
This is the running example with \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2)\) taken to its limit: as \(\tau^2 \to \infty\), the posterior mean \(\to x\), posterior variance \(\to \sigma^2\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Normal scale – it works
\(X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\), \(\pi(\sigma) = \sigma^{-1}\), \(n \geq 1\). \[
m(x) = \int_0^\infty \sigma^{-n}(2\pi)^{-n/2} \exp\!\left\{-\frac{\sum x_i^2}{2\sigma^2}\right\}\sigma^{-1}\, d\sigma.
\] Substitution \(u = 1/\sigma^2\): Gamma integral, finite when \(n \geq 1\) and \(\sum x_i^2 > 0\).
Posterior for \(\sigma^2\): Inverse-Gamma. Proper.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
A case where it fails
\(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \theta^2)\) with \(\theta > 0\) (the mean equals the standard deviation). Prior \(\pi(\theta) = \theta^{-1}\). \[
m(x) = \int_0^\infty \frac{1}{\theta^2\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left\{-\frac{(x - \theta)^2}{2\theta^2}\right\} d\theta.
\] The substitution \(u = x/\theta\) transforms this to an integral that diverges. The formal posterior is improper, and the Bayes computation is not valid.
This is a case where the “natural” noninformative prior fails.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Posterior expected loss (Berger, Definition 8)
The posterior expected loss of an action \(a\), when the posterior is \(\pi(\theta \mid x)\): \[
\rho(\pi(\theta \mid x),\, a) = \int_\Theta L(\theta, a)\, dF^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}(\theta).
\]
A (posterior) Bayes action \(\delta^\pi(x)\) is any action \(a \in \mathcal{A}\) that minimizes \(\rho(\pi(\theta \mid x), a)\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
section: Normal form equals extensive form (Berger, Result 1)
text:
Normal form equals extensive form (Berger, Result 1)
Result 1. A Bayes rule \(\delta^\pi\) (minimizing \(r(\pi, \delta)\)) can be found by choosing, for each \(x\) with \(m(x) > 0\), an action minimizing the posterior expected loss.
Proof. By Fubini’s theorem (applicable since \(L(\theta, a) \geq -K > -\infty\)): \[
r(\pi, \delta) = \int_\Theta R(\theta, \delta)\, dF^\pi(\theta) = \int_{\{x:\, m(x)>0\}} \rho(\pi(\theta \mid x),\, \delta(x))\, dm(x).
\] Minimize the integrand pointwise in \(x\). \(\square\)
The normal form (minimizing \(r(\pi, \delta)\) over all rules) and the extensive form (minimizing posterior expected loss for each \(x\)) give the same answer. We will virtually always work in the extensive form.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Generalized Bayes rules (Berger, Definition 9)
If \(\pi\) is an improper prior but \(\delta^\pi(x)\) minimizes the posterior expected loss for each \(x\) with \(m(x) > 0\), then \(\delta^\pi\) is called a generalized Bayes rule.
Generalized Bayes rules inherit the computational machinery of Bayesian decision theory. Their decision-theoretic properties (admissibility, minimaxity) are more delicate.
We study this in the next lecture (Section 4.8).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Squared-error loss: posterior mean (Berger, Result 3)
Result 3. If \(L(\theta, a) = (\theta - a)^2\), the Bayes rule is \[
\delta^\pi(x) = \mathbb{E}^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}[\theta].
\]
Proof. Differentiate \(\mathbb{E}^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}[(\theta - a)^2]\) with respect to \(a\): \[
-2\,\mathbb{E}^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}[\theta] + 2a = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad a = \mathbb{E}^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}[\theta]. \quad \square
\]
For quadratic loss \(L(\theta, a) = (\theta - a)'Q(\theta - a)\) with \(Q\) positive definite, the Bayes estimator is still the posterior mean. The matrix \(Q\) has no effect on the point estimate.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Running example under squared-error loss
\(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\), prior \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2)\). The posterior mean is \[
\delta^\pi(x) = \frac{\tau^2}{\sigma^2 + \tau^2}\, x + \frac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2 + \tau^2}\, \mu.
\] Weighted average, shrinking \(x\) toward \(\mu\). The shrinkage depends on \(\tau^2/\sigma^2\): the more spread the prior, the less shrinkage.
Under the noninformative prior \(\pi(\theta) = 1\): formal posterior is \(\mathcal{N}(x, \sigma^2)\), generalized Bayes estimator is \(\delta^0(x) = x\). The sample mean. The noninformative prior recovers it automatically.
This is Example 4 from Lecture 1 with \(c = \tau^2/(\sigma^2 + \tau^2)\): the Bayes rule under a normal prior gives the “optimal shrinkage” we could not determine there.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Absolute-error loss: posterior median (Berger, Result 5)
Result 5. If \(L(\theta, a) = |\theta - a|\), any median of \(\pi(\theta \mid x)\) is a Bayes estimator.
Proof. Let \(m\) be a median, \(a > m\). Then \[
L(\theta, m) - L(\theta, a) \leq (m-a)\,\mathbf{1}_{(-\infty, m]}(\theta) + (a-m)\,\mathbf{1}_{(m, \infty)}(\theta).
\] Posterior expectation: \[
\leq (m-a)\cdot P(\theta \leq m \mid x) + (a-m)\cdot P(\theta > m \mid x) \leq 0
\] since \(P(\theta \leq m \mid x) \geq 1/2\). Symmetric argument for \(a < m\). \(\square\)
When the posterior is unimodal and symmetric, the posterior mean and median coincide. The Bayes estimator is the same under squared-error and absolute-error loss.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Linear loss: posterior fractile (Berger, Result 6)
Result 6. If \[
L(\theta, a) = \begin{cases} K_0(\theta - a) & \text{if } \theta \geq a \\ K_1(a - \theta) & \text{if } \theta < a, \end{cases}
\] any \(\bigl(K_0/(K_0 + K_1)\bigr)\)-fractile of \(\pi(\theta \mid x)\) is a Bayes estimator.
\(K_0 = K_1\): the \(1/2\)-fractile is the median (recovering Result 5).
\(K_0 > K_1\) (underestimation is worse): Bayes estimator is above the median.
\(K_0 < K_1\) (overestimation is worse): Bayes estimator is below the median.

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Hypothesis testing under 0–1 loss (Berger $$4.4.3)
Test \(H_0: \theta \in \Theta_0\) vs. \(H_1: \theta \in \Theta_1\). Actions: \(a_0\) (accept \(H_0\)), \(a_1\) (reject \(H_0\)).
Under 0–1 loss: \[
\mathbb{E}^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}[L(\theta, a_1)] = P(\Theta_0 \mid x), \qquad \mathbb{E}^{\pi(\theta \mid x)}[L(\theta, a_0)] = P(\Theta_1 \mid x).
\]
Bayes action: accept the hypothesis with the larger posterior probability.
Under weighted 0–1 loss (\(K_0\) for Type I, \(K_1\) for Type II): accept \(H_0\) if \[
K_0\, P(\Theta_0 \mid x) \leq K_1\, P(\Theta_1 \mid x),
\] equivalently if the posterior odds exceed \(K_0/K_1\).

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Summary
Noninformative priors (Berger $$3.3):

Location: \(\pi(\theta) = 1\). Scale: \(\pi(\sigma) = \sigma^{-1}\). Location-scale: \(\pi(\theta, \sigma) = \sigma^{-1}\).
General: Jeffreys prior \(\pi(\theta) = [I(\theta)]^{1/2}\), reparameterization invariant.
Improper, can fail (improper posteriors), but succeed in most standard problems.

Bayesian decision theory (Berger $$4.4):

Posterior expected loss \(\to\) minimize pointwise in \(x\) (extensive form = normal form).
Squared-error \(\to\) posterior mean. Absolute-error \(\to\) posterior median. 0–1 loss \(\to\) larger posterior probability.
Generalized Bayes rules: same machinery, but admissibility not guaranteed.

Next lecture: When is a Bayes rule admissible, and when does it fail? (Berger $$4.8.)

title: Noninformative Priors and Bayesian Decision Theory
text:
Reading
Berger, Section 3.3; Sections 4.1–4.4.
Exercises: Berger 3.3 Exercises 8–17; Berger 4.4 Exercises 41, 43, 44.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Recap
In Lectures 1–2 we built the decision-theoretic framework: \(\Theta\), \(\mathcal{A}\), \(L(\theta, a)\), \(\delta\), \(R(\theta, \delta)\), \(\rho(\pi, a)\), \(r(\pi, \delta)\), admissibility, three selection principles, randomized rules.
We proved three structural results: sufficiency reduces the problem (Theorem 1), convex loss eliminates randomization (Theorem 3), and the Rao–Blackwell theorem combines them.
Throughout, we used the loss function \(L(\theta, a)\) without asking two questions:

Where does the loss function come from?
Why is expected loss the right way to aggregate it?

Today we address both. Reading: Berger, Chapter 2 (except Section 2.3).

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Two problems with “value”
In evaluating consequences of actions, two problems arise:
1. Some consequences have no natural scale. Prestige, customer goodwill, reputation – important to businesses, but hard to quantify. A company considering selling its name-brand product in discount stores can estimate the immediate profit, but the long-term effect on prestige is much harder to measure.
2. Even when a monetary scale exists, it may not reflect true value. Suppose you can do an unpleasant task for $100. At your current income, you might do it. After receiving a million dollars, you probably would not. The value of $100 depends on context.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: Value is not linear in money
text:
Value is not linear in money
Suppose you are offered a choice:
\(\quad\) (A) Receive $200,000 for certain.
\(\quad\) (B) A 50–50 chance of receiving $0 or $410,000.
The expected monetary value of (B) is $205,000 \(>\) $200,000. Yet most people prefer (A).
This means the “value” of money is not linear in the amount. The marginal value of money is decreasing: the difference in value between $0 and $200,000 is larger than the difference between $200,000 and $410,000.
We need a way to assign numerical values to consequences that reflects the decision maker’s true preferences. That is what utility theory does.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
The setup
Let \(\mathcal{R}\) be the set of all possible consequences (rewards). Let \(\mathcal{P}\) be the set of all probability distributions on \(\mathcal{R}\).
We assume the decision maker can state preferences among elements of \(\mathcal{P}\):

\(P_1 \prec P_2\): \(P_2\) is preferred to \(P_1\).
\(P_1 \approx P_2\): \(P_1\) and \(P_2\) are equivalent.
\(P_1 \preceq P_2\): \(P_1\) is not preferred to \(P_2\).

A reward \(r \in \mathcal{R}\) is identified with the degenerate distribution \((r)\) that puts probability one on \(r\). So preferences apply to rewards as well.
The goal: find a function \(U: \mathcal{R}\to \mathbb{R}\) such that \(P_1 \prec P_2 \iff E^{P_1}[U(r)] < E^{P_2}[U(r)]\). Such a \(U\) is called a utility function.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Constructing a utility function
Assume a utility function exists. Here is one way to build it.
Step 1. Choose two rewards \(r_1 \prec r_2\). Set \(U(r_1) = 0\), \(U(r_2) = 1\). (This fixes the scale.)
Step 2. For any \(r_3\) with \(r_1 \prec r_3 \prec r_2\): find \(\alpha \in (0,1)\) such that \(r_3 \approx \alpha(r_1) + (1-\alpha)(r_2)\). Then \(U(r_3) = 1 - \alpha\).
The idea: \(r_3\) is equivalent to a gamble between \(r_1\) (prob. \(\alpha\)) and \(r_2\) (prob. \(1-\alpha\)). Then \(U(r_3) = \alpha U(r_1) + (1-\alpha)U(r_2) = 1-\alpha\).
Step 3. For \(r_3 \prec r_1\): find \(\alpha\) such that \(r_1 \approx \alpha(r_3) + (1-\alpha)(r_2)\). Then \(0 = \alpha U(r_3) + (1-\alpha)\), giving \(U(r_3) = -(1-\alpha)/\alpha < 0\).
Step 4. For \(r_2 \prec r_3\): find \(\alpha\) such that \(r_2 \approx \alpha(r_1) + (1-\alpha)(r_3)\). Then \(1 = (1-\alpha)U(r_3)\), giving \(U(r_3) = 1/(1-\alpha) > 1\).
Step 5 (Consistency). If \(U(r_3), U(r_4), U(r_5)\) are found with \(r_3 \prec r_4 \prec r_5\), find \(\alpha\) with \(r_4 \approx \alpha(r_3) + (1-\alpha)(r_5)\). Check: \(U(r_4) = \alpha U(r_3) + (1-\alpha)U(r_5)\).

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: Example: football or movie? (Berger, Ex. 1) – setup
text:
Example: football or movie? (Berger, Ex. 1) – setup
Saturday afternoon: \(a_1\) = football game, \(a_2\) = movie. There is a 40% chance of rain (\(\theta_1\)).
Four possible outcomes (action, weather):








\(r_1 = (a_1, \theta_1)\):
football, rain
(ruined afternoon – worst)


\(r_2 = (a_1, \theta_2)\):
football, no rain
(great day – best)


\(r_3 = (a_2, \theta_1)\):
movie, rain
(good movie, smart choice)


\(r_4 = (a_2, \theta_2)\):
movie, no rain
(okay, slight regret)



Preference ordering: \(r_1 \prec r_4 \prec r_3 \prec r_2\).
Fix the scale: \(U(r_1) = 0\), \(U(r_2) = 1\). We need to determine \(U(r_3)\) and \(U(r_4)\).

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: Determining the utilities: two subjective judgments
text:
Determining the utilities: two subjective judgments
To find \(U(r_4)\) and \(U(r_3)\), the decision maker asks: “at what gamble between \(r_1\) (worst) and \(r_2\) (best) am I indifferent to this reward?”
Judgment 1: “I am indifferent between \(r_4\) (movie, no rain) and a gamble that gives \(r_1\) with probability 0.4 and \(r_2\) with probability 0.6.” That is: \(r_4 \approx 0.4(r_1) + 0.6(r_2)\).
This gives \(U(r_4) = 0.4 \cdot 0 + 0.6 \cdot 1 = 0.6\).
Judgment 2: “I am indifferent between \(r_3\) (movie, rain) and a gamble that gives \(r_1\) with probability 0.3 and \(r_2\) with probability 0.7.” That is: \(r_3 \approx 0.3(r_1) + 0.7(r_2)\).
This gives \(U(r_3) = 0.3 \cdot 0 + 0.7 \cdot 1 = 0.7\).
Both are subjective: they come from introspection. Different people could reasonably give different numbers.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: The consistency check: a third judgment that is not free
text:
The consistency check: a third judgment that is not free
We now have \(U(r_1) = 0\), \(U(r_4) = 0.6\), \(U(r_3) = 0.7\), \(U(r_2) = 1\).
Judgment 3: The decision maker compares \(r_3\) to a gamble between \(r_4\) and \(r_2\). “At what \(\alpha\) am I indifferent between \(r_3\) and \(\alpha(r_4) + (1-\alpha)(r_2)\)?” After reflection: \(\alpha = 0.6\).
But if the utility function from Judgments 1 and 2 is correct: \[
U(r_3) = \alpha\, U(r_4) + (1-\alpha)\, U(r_2) \quad \implies \quad 0.7 = 0.6\alpha + (1-\alpha) \quad \implies \quad \alpha = 0.75.
\]
The decision maker said \(\alpha = 0.6\), but the first two judgments force \(\alpha = 0.75\). At least one of the three judgments is wrong.
This is the point: once two utilities are fixed, the third comparison is determined. If it disagrees, the decision maker must revise. Suppose the revised values are \(U(r_4) = 0.6\) and \(U(r_3) = 0.75\).

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Making the decision
With the consistent utilities \(U(r_1) = 0\), \(U(r_3) = 0.75\), \(U(r_4) = 0.6\), \(U(r_2) = 1\):
Expected utility of each action: \[
\begin{aligned}
a_1 \text{ (football)}: \quad &\pi(\theta_1)\, U(r_1) + \pi(\theta_2)\, U(r_2) = (0.4)(0) + (0.6)(1) = 0.6. \\
a_2 \text{ (movie)}: \quad &\pi(\theta_1)\, U(r_3) + \pi(\theta_2)\, U(r_4) = (0.4)(0.75) + (0.6)(0.6) = 0.66.
\end{aligned}
\]
The optimal action: go to the movie.
The raw payoffs might have suggested football (higher upside). But the utility function encodes an asymmetry: a ruined afternoon (\(U = 0\)) is much worse than the mild regret of missing the game when it did not rain (\(U = 0.6\), not that bad). That asymmetry tips the balance.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
The axioms
For the construction to work and for \(U\) to represent preferences, the preference ordering must be “rational.” Four axioms:
Axiom 1 (Completeness): For any \(P_1, P_2 \in \mathcal{P}\), either \(P_1 \prec P_2\), \(P_1 \approx P_2\), or \(P_2 \prec P_1\).
Axiom 2 (Transitivity): If \(P_1 \preceq P_2\) and \(P_2 \preceq P_3\), then \(P_1 \preceq P_3\).
Axiom 3 (Independence): If \(P_1 \prec P_2\), then \(\alpha P_1 + (1-\alpha)P_3 \prec \alpha P_2 + (1-\alpha)P_3\) for any \(0 < \alpha < 1\) and \(P_3 \in \mathcal{P}\).
Axiom 4 (Continuity): If \(P_1 \prec P_2 \prec P_3\), there exist \(0 < \alpha, \beta < 1\) such that \(\alpha P_1 + (1-\alpha) P_3 \prec P_2\) and \(P_2 \prec \beta P_1 + (1-\beta) P_3\).

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Interpreting the axioms
Axioms 1 and 2 are housekeeping: you can compare anything, and your comparisons are transitive.
Axiom 3 (independence): if you prefer \(P_2\) to \(P_1\), mixing both with the same \(P_3\) does not reverse the preference. Replacing \(P_1\) by \(P_2\) in one component of a mixture is always an improvement.
Axiom 4 (continuity): no consequence is infinitely good or infinitely bad. If \(P_1\) were infinitely bad (say, death), there would be no \(\beta > 0\) for which you would risk it to get \(P_3\) instead of \(P_2\). But if death were truly infinitely bad, you would never cross a street, never drive a car. People do these things, so Axiom 4 holds in practice.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
The existence theorem and uniqueness
Theorem. If the preference ordering on \(\mathcal{P}\) satisfies Axioms 1–4, there exists a utility function \(U: \mathcal{R}\to \mathbb{R}\) such that \(P_1 \prec P_2 \iff E^{P_1}[U(r)] < E^{P_2}[U(r)]\).
We will not prove existence. The proof constructs \(U\) using the procedure from earlier and verifies it represents the preferences; see DeGroot (1970). What we will prove are two properties of \(U\) that we will use.
Uniqueness up to affine transformation. If \(U\) is a utility function, so is \(V = bU + c\) for any \(b > 0\). Proof: \[
\begin{aligned}
E^{P_1}[U(r)] < E^{P_2}[U(r)] &\iff b\,E^{P_1}[U(r)] + c < b\,E^{P_2}[U(r)] + c \\
&\iff E^{P_1}[V(r)] < E^{P_2}[V(r)].
\end{aligned}
\] The preferences are unchanged. \(\square\)
This means \(U(r_1) = 0\), \(U(r_2) = 1\) is just a convention. Any two reference points give an equivalent utility function.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Axiom 4 implies boundedness
Proposition. If the preference ordering satisfies Axioms 1–4 and \(U\) is the resulting utility function, then \(U\) is bounded.
Proof. Suppose for contradiction that \(U\) is unbounded above: there exist rewards \(s_1, s_2, \ldots \in \mathcal{R}\) with \(U(s_n) \to \infty\).
Choose weights \(p_n > 0\) with \(\sum p_n = 1\) and \(\sum p_n U(s_n) = \infty\). Define \(Q = \sum p_n (s_n) \in \mathcal{P}\).
Pick any two rewards \(a \prec b\). Since \(E^Q[U(r)] = \infty > U(b) > U(a)\): \((a) \prec (b) \prec Q\).
Axiom 4 says: there exists \(\alpha \in (0,1)\) such that \(\alpha(a) + (1-\alpha)Q \prec (b)\), i.e., \(E^{\alpha(a) + (1-\alpha)Q}[U(r)] < U(b)\).
But for any \(\alpha \in (0,1)\): \[
E^{\alpha(a)+(1-\alpha)Q}[U(r)] = \alpha\, U(a) + (1-\alpha)\cdot \infty = \infty > U(b).
\]
No such \(\alpha\) exists. Contradiction. Symmetric argument for unbounded below. \(\square\)

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Properties of monetary utility functions
When \(\mathcal{R}\) is monetary, \(U(r)\) typically has four features:
(i) Linear for small \(r\). You would take a fair coin flip on \(\pm 25\) cents. So \(U\) is linear near \(r = 0\). The “small” range depends on context: 25 cents for a student, millions for a large corporation.
(ii) Concave for \(r > 0\). Marginal utility \(U'(r)\) is decreasing, so \(U''(r) < 0\). An additional $100 matters more at $0 than at $1,000,000.
(iii) Different shape for gains and losses. People are often risk-seeking for losses (willing to gamble to avoid a sure loss) but risk-averse for gains. So \(U(r)\) may be convex for \(r < 0\) and concave for \(r > 0\).
(iv) Bounded. Few would assign meaningful additional utility to money beyond $10\(^{100}\).

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Defining the loss function
Each pair \((\theta, a)\) determines a consequence with utility \(U(\theta, a)\). The loss function is \[
L(\theta, a) = -U(\theta, a).
\]
Maximizing expected utility is the same as minimizing expected loss.
This is the answer to our second question: why is expected loss the right criterion? Because the utility axioms tell us that expected utility is the correct measure of value under uncertainty. Expected loss inherits this justification.
The Bayesian expected loss \(\rho(\pi, a) = E_\pi[L(\theta, a)]\), the risk \(R(\theta, \delta) = E_{P_\theta}[L(\theta, \delta(X))]\), the Bayes risk \(r(\pi, \delta)\), and the loss of a randomized rule – all are justified by this construction.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
When consequences are random
Often \((\theta, a)\) does not directly determine the consequence. The reward is a random variable \(Z\) whose distribution depends on \(\theta\) and \(a\). Then: \[
U(\theta, a) = E_{\theta, a}[U(Z)], \qquad L(\theta, a) = -E_{\theta, a}[U(Z)].
\]
One must be careful to define \(Z\) as the overall outcome.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Properties of loss functions
1. Invariance under positive affine transformations.
Claim: If \(L\) is a valid loss, then \(L' = bL + c\) (with \(b > 0\)) leads to the same optimal decisions.
Proof (for the Bayes case): The Bayes risk is \(r(\pi, \delta) = E_\pi[R(\theta, \delta)]\). Under \(L'\): \[
r'(\pi, \delta) = E_\pi[E_{P_\theta}[bL(\theta, \delta(X)) + c]] = b\, r(\pi, \delta) + c.
\] Since \(b > 0\), the \(\delta\) minimizing \(r'\) is the same as the \(\delta\) minimizing \(r\). \(\square\)
So we can always shift: \(L^*(\theta, a) = L(\theta, a) - \inf_{\theta, a} L(\theta, a) \geq 0\).
2. Regret loss. \(\ell(\theta, a) = \sup_{a'} U(\theta, a') - U(\theta, a)\): the loss relative to the best action for the true \(\theta\). Equivalent to \(L\) for Bayesian analysis, but can differ for minimax. We return to this in Chapter 5.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
I. Squared-error loss
\[
L(\theta, a) = (\theta - a)^2.
\]
Why is it popular?

For unbiased estimators, \(R(\theta, \delta) = \mathrm{Var}_{P_\theta}(\delta(X))\).
Related to least squares theory.
Calculations are often tractable.

Berger’s honest assessment: “The above justifications really have very little merit. The question is – does squared-error loss typically reflect the true loss?”
Squared-error loss is unbounded and convex: large errors are penalized very severely. In many real problems, the loss should be bounded and (for large errors) concave.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: Example: risk of the sample mean under squared-error loss
text:
Example: risk of the sample mean under squared-error loss
\(X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\). Estimate \(\theta\) with \(\delta(X) = \bar{X}\).
\[
R(\theta, \delta) = E_{P_\theta}[(\theta - \bar{X})^2] = \mathrm{Var}_{P_\theta}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}.
\]
The risk is constant in \(\theta\) and decreases in \(n\).
Now consider \(\delta_c(X) = c\bar{X}\) for some \(c \in \mathbb{R}\). Then: \[
R(\theta, \delta_c) = E_{P_\theta}[(\theta - c\bar{X})^2] = c^2 \frac{\sigma^2}{n} + (1-c)^2\theta^2.
\]
This is Example 4 from Lecture 1 with \(\sigma^2/n\) replacing 1. Minimizing over \(c\) for a fixed \(\theta\) gives \(c^* = \theta^2/(\theta^2 + \sigma^2/n)\), which depends on \(\theta\) – so the “optimal shrinkage” is unknowable. This tension is at the heart of the course.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: The Taylor expansion defense of squared-error loss
text:
The Taylor expansion defense of squared-error loss
Suppose the reward is \(Z = h(\theta - a, Y)\), where \(Y\) is an extraneous random variable independent of \(\theta\) and \(a\).
Define \(g(\theta - a, Y) = U(h(\theta - a, Y))\). If the sample information is accurate, \(\theta - a\) is small. Expand in Taylor series around 0: \[
g(\theta - a, Y) \approx g(0, Y) + (\theta - a)g'(0, Y) + \tfrac{1}{2}(\theta - a)^2 g''(0, Y).
\]
Taking expectations over \(Y\), with \(K_1 = -E_Y[g(0,Y)]\), \(K_2 = -E_Y[g'(0,Y)]\), \(K_3 = -\frac{1}{2}E_Y[g''(0,Y)]\): \[
L(\theta, a) \approx K_1 + K_2(\theta - a) + K_3(\theta - a)^2.
\]
Completing the square (assuming \(K_3 > 0\)): \[
L(\theta, a) \approx K_3\!\left(\theta - a + \frac{K_2}{2K_3}\right)^{\!2} + \text{const}.
\]
When \(K_2 = 0\) (symmetric utility, symmetric \(Y\)), this is squared-error loss.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Variants of squared-error loss
Weighted squared-error: \(L(\theta, a) = w(\theta)(\theta - a)^2\).
The weight \(w(\theta) > 0\) encodes that a given error may matter more at some parameter values than others.
Example: estimating a Poisson rate \(\theta > 0\). An error of 1 when \(\theta = 0.5\) is much worse than the same error when \(\theta = 100\). The weight \(w(\theta) = 1/\theta^2\) normalizes for scale, giving \(L(\theta, a) = (1 - a/\theta)^2\).
Quadratic loss (multivariate): \(\theta, a \in \mathbb{R}^p\), \(Q\) positive definite: \[
L(\theta, a) = (\theta - a)^T Q (\theta - a).
\] When \(Q = I_p\): \(L(\theta, a) = \|\theta - a\|^2 = \sum_{i=1}^p (\theta_i - a_i)^2\).
We will encounter this extensively when studying Stein estimation in Week 6.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Example: risk under sum-of-squares loss
\(X = (X_1, \ldots, X_p)^T\) with \(X_i \overset{\text{ind}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\theta_i, 1)\). Estimate \(\theta = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_p)^T\) under \(L(\theta, a) = \|\theta - a\|^2\).
The natural estimator is \(\delta(X) = X\). Its risk: \[
R(\theta, \delta) = E_{P_\theta}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^p (\theta_i - X_i)^2\right] = \sum_{i=1}^p \mathrm{Var}_{P_\theta}(X_i) = p.
\]
Constant risk \(p\), for all \(\theta\). Seems hard to improve.
For \(p \geq 3\), James and Stein (1960) showed the estimator \[
\delta^{JS}(X) = \left(1 - \frac{p-2}{\|X\|^2}\right)X
\] satisfies \(R(\theta, \delta^{JS}) < p\) for all \(\theta\). The sample mean vector is inadmissible.
That result is one of the major topics later in the course.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
II. Linear loss
When the utility function is approximately linear over the relevant range, the loss is approximately linear: \[
L(\theta, a) = \begin{cases} K_0(\theta - a) & \text{if } \theta - a \geq 0 \quad \text{(underestimation)} \\ K_1(a - \theta) & \text{if } \theta - a < 0 \quad \text{(overestimation)} \end{cases}
\]
\(K_0\) and \(K_1\) encode the relative cost of under- vs. overestimation.
When \(K_0 = K_1\): absolute error loss \(L(\theta, a) = |\theta - a|\).
Recall Lecture 1, Example 1 (drug company): \(L(\theta_2, a) = (\theta_2 - a)\) if \(\theta_2 \geq a\) and \(2(a - \theta_2)\) if \(\theta_2 < a\). That is linear loss with \(K_0 = 1\), \(K_1 = 2\): overestimating the adverse reaction rate costs twice as much as underestimating.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: Example: car production under linear loss (Berger, Ex. 17)
text:
Example: car production under linear loss (Berger, Ex. 17)
A company must decide how many cars \(a\) to produce. The demand is \(\theta\) (unknown). Each car sold yields $500 profit. Each unsold car yields $1000 loss.
If \(a \leq \theta\): all cars sell, profit = \(500a\). If \(a > \theta\): \(\theta\) cars sell, \(a - \theta\) do not, profit = \(500\theta - 1000(a - \theta) = 1500\theta - 1000a\).
The regret loss: the best action is \(a^* = \theta\), profit \(500\theta\). So: \[
\ell(\theta, a) = 500\theta - \text{profit}(a) = \begin{cases} 500(\theta - a) & \text{if } a \leq \theta \\ 1500(a - \theta) & \text{if } a > \theta \end{cases}
\]
Linear loss with \(K_0 = 500\) (opportunity cost of underproduction) and \(K_1 = 1500\) (cost of overproduction). The asymmetry \(K_1/K_0 = 3\) reflects that unsold cars are much worse than unmet demand.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
III. “0–1” loss
In a two-action problem (testing \(H_0: \theta \in \Theta_0\) vs. \(H_1: \theta \in \Theta_1\)): \[
L(\theta, a_i) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \theta \in \Theta_i \quad \text{(correct)} \\ 1 & \text{if } \theta \in \Theta_j,\ j \neq i \quad \text{(incorrect)} \end{cases}
\]
The risk function:

When \(\theta \in \Theta_0\): \(R(\theta, \delta) = P_{P_\theta}(\text{reject } H_0)\) = Type I error.
When \(\theta \in \Theta_1\): \(R(\theta, \delta) = P_{P_\theta}(\text{accept } H_0)\) = Type II error.

The Bayesian expected loss: \(\rho(\pi^*, a_i) = 1 - \pi^*(\theta \in \Theta_i)\).
The error probabilities from Neyman–Pearson theory are the risk function under 0–1 loss. Testing is a special case of decision theory.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: Example: 0–1 loss for a normal test
text:
Example: 0–1 loss for a normal test
\(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, 1)\). Test \(H_0: \theta \leq 0\) vs. \(H_1: \theta > 0\). Decision rule: reject \(H_0\) if \(X > c\).
Under 0–1 loss, the risk function is: \[
R(\theta, \delta_c) = \begin{cases}
P_{P_\theta}(X > c) = 1 - \Phi(c - \theta) & \text{if } \theta \leq 0 \quad \text{(Type I)} \\
P_{P_\theta}(X \leq c) = \Phi(c - \theta) & \text{if } \theta > 0 \quad \text{(Type II)}
\end{cases}
\]
For \(c = 0\): Type I error at \(\theta = 0\) is \(1 - \Phi(0) = 0.5\). Type II error at \(\theta = 1\) is \(\Phi(-1) = 0.159\).
For \(c = 1.645\): Type I error at \(\theta = 0\) is \(1 - \Phi(1.645) = 0.05\). Type II error at \(\theta = 1\) is \(\Phi(0.645) = 0.74\).
The familiar tradeoff: making \(R(\theta, \delta_c)\) smaller for \(\theta \leq 0\) makes it larger for \(\theta > 0\), and vice versa.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Weighted 0–1 loss
Basic 0–1 loss treats the two types of error as equally costly. Rarely appropriate.
More realistic: \[
L(\theta, a_i) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if correct} \\ K_0 & \text{if reject } H_0 \text{ when } \theta \in \Theta_0 \quad \text{(Type I)} \\ K_1 & \text{if accept } H_0 \text{ when } \theta \in \Theta_1 \quad \text{(Type II)} \end{cases}
\]
The risk becomes \(K_0 \cdot P_{P_\theta}(\text{reject } H_0)\) for \(\theta \in \Theta_0\) and \(K_1 \cdot P_{P_\theta}(\text{accept } H_0)\) for \(\theta \in \Theta_1\).
Even more generally: \(L(\theta, a_1) = K_0(\theta)\) for \(\theta \in \Theta_0\), where the weight varies within \(\Theta_0\). A Type I error may be worse when \(\theta\) is far from the boundary.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Inference as a decision problem
The most common use: representing standard inference measures as a risk or Bayesian expected loss.
Confidence sets. Let \(C(x) \subseteq \Theta\) be a confidence rule. The loss \[
L(\theta, C(x)) = 1 - I_{C(x)}(\theta) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \theta \in C(x) \\ 1 & \text{if } \theta \notin C(x) \end{cases}
\] gives \(R(\theta, C) = 1 - P_{P_\theta}(C(X) \ni \theta)\): one minus the coverage probability.
Unbiased estimation. Restrict to \(E_{P_\theta}[\delta(X)] = \theta\) for all \(\theta\). Under squared-error loss, \(R(\theta, \delta) = \mathrm{Var}_{P_\theta}(\delta(X))\). The UMVUE is the decision-theoretic optimum within this restricted class.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: Scoring rules: quantifying how well confidence works (Berger, Ex. 4)
text:
Scoring rules: quantifying how well confidence works (Berger, Ex. 4)
A confidence rule \(C(x)\) with associated “confidence” \(\alpha(x)\): after observing \(x\), we report \(\alpha(x)\) as our confidence that \(\theta \in C(x)\). How well does \(\alpha(x)\) reflect reality?
The Brier scoring rule: \[
L_C(\theta, \alpha(x)) = (\alpha(x) - I_{C(x)}(\theta))^2.
\]
When \(\theta \in C(x)\): loss = \((\alpha(x) - 1)^2\), small if \(\alpha(x) \approx 1\).
When \(\theta \notin C(x)\): loss = \(\alpha(x)^2\), small if \(\alpha(x) \approx 0\).

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
section: Scoring rules continued: constant vs. data-dependent confidence
text:
Scoring rules continued: constant vs. data-dependent confidence
Consider two choices: \(\alpha_1(x) \equiv 0.75\) (constant frequentist confidence) and \(\alpha_2(x) = P(\theta \in C(x) \mid x)\) (posterior probability, data-dependent confidence).
The risk of \(\alpha_1\) under the Brier loss: \[
R(\theta, \alpha_1) = E_{P_\theta}\!\left[(\alpha_1(X) - I_{C(X)}(\theta))^2\right] = 0.75^2 \cdot P_{P_\theta}(\theta \notin C(X)) + 0.25^2 \cdot P_{P_\theta}(\theta \in C(X)).
\]
If \(C\) has 75% coverage: \(R(\theta, \alpha_1) = (0.5625)(0.25) + (0.0625)(0.75) = 0.1875\).
The data-dependent confidence \(\alpha_2\) adapts: it reports high confidence when the data are informative and low confidence when they are not. One can show (and we will, in Chapter 4) that \(R(\theta, \alpha_2) \leq R(\theta, \alpha_1)\) for all \(\theta\), with strict inequality for some \(\theta\).
The data-dependent confidence dominates the constant one.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Predictive problems
Predict a future observation \(Z\) with density \(g(z|\theta)\), given data \(X\) with density \(f(x|\theta)\).
If the original loss is \(L^*(z, a)\), the decision-theoretic reduction is: \[
L(\theta, a) = E_{P_\theta}[L^*(Z, a)] = \int L^*(z, a)\, g(z|\theta)\, dz.
\]
This reduces the prediction problem to a standard decision problem involving \(\theta\).

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Example: predicting a normal (Berger, Ex. 5)
\(Z \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)\), \(L^*(z, a) = (z - a)^2\). Then: \[
\begin{aligned}
L(\theta, a) &= E_{P_\theta}[(Z - a)^2] = E_{P_\theta}[(Z - \theta)^2 + 2(Z - \theta)(\theta - a) + (\theta - a)^2] \\
&= \mathrm{Var}_{P_\theta}(Z) + 0 + (\theta - a)^2 = \sigma^2 + (\theta - a)^2.
\end{aligned}
\]
The cross term vanishes because \(E_{P_\theta}[Z - \theta] = 0\).
The constant \(\sigma^2\) does not affect the choice of \(a\). Choosing the best prediction of \(Z\) is equivalent to estimating \(\theta\) under squared-error loss.
This partially explains why decision theorists focus on estimating unknown parameters rather than predicting future observations: prediction problems often reduce to parameter estimation through the loss.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Vector-valued loss functions (Berger $$2.4.5)
It is common to evaluate procedures by several criteria simultaneously. Confidence sets are judged by (size, coverage). Sequential analysis deals with (sample size, decision loss). In medical problems one may face (“death of a patient”, “cost of a drug”) – consequences that are very hard to compare.
The practical response. Carry the components separately. If one action dominates on all components, the choice is clear. If not, perform the utility comparison only when needed. Initial subjective inputs may be crude, with refinements made only if necessary.
The theoretical reduction (Cohen and Sackrowitz, 1984). Given a vector loss \(L = (L_1, \ldots, L_J)\), treat the component index \(i\) as a parameter. Define: \[
L^*((\theta, i),\, a) = L_i(\theta, a).
\] This \(L^*\) is real-valued. Risk dominance with respect to \(L^*\) (for all \(\theta\) and all \(i\)) corresponds exactly to vector risk dominance. So decision-theoretic results established for scalar losses transfer directly to vector-valued losses.

title: Utility Theory and the Loss Function
text:
Criticisms (Berger $$2.5)
The use of loss functions is criticized on three grounds.
(i) Inappropriate for inference. Some statisticians argue that inference problems (estimation, testing, confidence sets) should not be forced into a decision-theoretic mold. We discussed this in Section 1.2 and Subsection 2.4.3. The moderate position: better inference can often be done with the aid of decision-theoretic machinery and inference losses. The decision-theoretic framework does not replace inference; it can sharpen it.
(ii) Difficult. Utility analyses can be very hard. The amount of effort depends on the importance of the problem and on the degree of refinement needed. If the conclusion is clear with only crude utility specifications, a detailed construction is unnecessary. If the loss does significantly affect the conclusion, there is no alternative to a careful utility analysis.
(iii) Nonrobust. Any loss used in practice is uncertain. Small changes in the loss function could, in principle, change the optimal action. The response: perform a sensitivity analysis. Show that reasonable variations in \(L\) do not markedly affect the conclusion. If they do, that is a signal that even more careful consideration of the loss is needed, not that the decision-theoretic approach is wrong.
